content.text: Breach Detection: From Violation to Incident

A data contract breach is any delivery from a producer that violates the contract's schema, quality expectations, or SLA terms. Breach detection is the automated monitoring step that identifies violations immediately on delivery and classifies them by severity and type so that the right remediation action is taken without delay.

The distinction between a minor anomaly and a breach matters: not every deviation requires a full breach response. A delivery that arrives 2 minutes late against a 5-minute SLA is within the tolerance buffer and should not trigger a formal breach incident. The breach detection system must apply the defined SLA thresholds and severity tiers, not flag every imperfection as a crisis.
Breach Classification and Severity Tiers
The diagram below maps breach types to severity tiers and their automated responses in the AI pipeline.

attrs.alt: Breach classification table mapping schema invalid to P1 hard block, completeness below threshold to P2 warn-but-ingest, and latency breach to P2/P3 with staleness flagging

content.text: The key design decision is the completeness breach response: warn-but-ingest rather than hard block. This is correct for most cases because blocking a 97% complete delivery when the threshold is 99% harms the AI agent more than allowing the partial delivery with flagged metadata. The exception is when the missing field is critical (e.g., product price) — these fields should trigger P1 hard block regardless of the overall completeness rate.
The Remediation Workflow

A data contract breach triggers a structured remediation workflow. Each step has a defined owner and a time limit. The workflow must be documented in the contract so both producer and consumer teams know their responsibilities before a breach occurs.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class RemediationStep:
    step_id: str
    name: str
    owner: Literal["producer", "consumer", "steward"]
    due_offset_minutes: int  # Minutes after breach detection
    description: str
    completed_at: datetime | None = None

STANDARD_REMEDIATION_WORKFLOW = [
    RemediationStep(
        step_id="notify", name="Breach Notification", owner="consumer",
        due_offset_minutes=5,
        description="Consumer monitoring system sends breach notification to producer via configured channel",
    ),
    RemediationStep(
        step_id="ack", name="Producer Acknowledgment", owner="producer",
        due_offset_minutes=30,  # P1 SLA
        description="Producer on-call acknowledges breach and provides initial root cause",
    ),
    RemediationStep(
        step_id="root_cause", name="Root Cause Analysis", owner="producer",
        due_offset_minutes=120,
        description="Producer identifies root cause and provides ETA for data restoration",
    ),
    RemediationStep(
        step_id="remediation_delivery", name="Remediation Data Delivery", owner="producer",
        due_offset_minutes=240,
        description="Producer delivers remediation batch covering all data missed during the breach window",
    ),
    RemediationStep(
        step_id="validation", name="Remediation Validation", owner="consumer",
        due_offset_minutes=300,
        description="Consumer pipeline validates the remediation batch against contract schema and quality",
    ),
    RemediationStep(
        step_id="unblock", name="Ingestion Unblock", owner="consumer",
        due_offset_minutes=360,
        description="Consumer pipeline resumes normal ingestion; producer source unblocked",
    ),
    RemediationStep(
        step_id="postmortem", name="Joint Postmortem", owner="steward",
        due_offset_minutes=2880,  # 48 hours
        description="Joint postmortem document with corrective actions and prevention measures",
    ),
]

def get_due_time(step: RemediationStep, breach_detected_at: datetime) -> datetime:
    return breach_detected_at + timedelta(minutes=step.due_offset_minutes)

The remediation_delivery step is the most technically complex. The producer must identify the exact time range of the breach (from breach start to resolution), query their source system for all records that changed during that window, and deliver them as a complete remediation batch. This requires the producer to maintain a change log or audit table in their source system, not just the current state.
Breach Event Lifecycle Tracking

Each breach must have an event record that tracks its full lifecycle from detection through resolution. This record is the audit trail for the breach and provides the data needed for SLA compliance reporting, postmortem analysis, and trend detection across multiple breaches.

The breach event record must capture the timeline of every key transition: when the breach was detected by automated monitoring, when the notification was sent to the producer, when the producer acknowledged, when root cause was identified, when the remediation batch was delivered, when ingestion was unblocked, and when the postmortem was completed. Any gap in this timeline indicates a missed SLA step that must be escalated.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class BreachLifecycleEvent:
    breach_id: str
    contract_id: str
    breach_type: Literal[
        "schema_invalid", "completeness_below_threshold",
        "latency_breach", "missed_delivery", "freshness_expired"
    ]
    severity: Literal["P1", "P2", "P3"]
    detected_at: datetime
    affected_domain: str          # product, employee, policy, etc.
    affected_field: str | None    # Specific field if field-level breach
    actual_value: float           # The measured value that violated the SLA
    threshold_value: float        # The SLA threshold that was violated

    # Timeline fields — populated as the breach progresses
    notification_sent_at: datetime | None = None
    producer_acked_at: datetime | None = None
    root_cause_provided_at: datetime | None = None
    root_cause_summary: str | None = None
    remediation_batch_delivered_at: datetime | None = None
    remediation_batch_id: str | None = None
    remediation_validated_at: datetime | None = None
    remediation_validation_passed: bool | None = None
    ingestion_unblocked_at: datetime | None = None
    postmortem_completed_at: datetime | None = None
    postmortem_url: str | None = None
    resolution_status: Literal["open", "resolved", "escalated"] = "open"

    def ack_sla_elapsed_minutes(self) -> float | None:
        if not self.notification_sent_at or not self.producer_acked_at:
            return None
        return (self.producer_acked_at - self.notification_sent_at).total_seconds() / 60

    def resolution_elapsed_minutes(self) -> float | None:
        if not self.ingestion_unblocked_at:
            return None
        return (self.ingestion_unblocked_at - self.detected_at).total_seconds() / 60

    def missing_steps(self) -> list[str]:
        """Return the names of workflow steps not yet completed."""
        steps = []
        if not self.notification_sent_at:
            steps.append("notify")
        if not self.producer_acked_at:
            steps.append("ack")
        if not self.root_cause_provided_at:
            steps.append("root_cause")
        if not self.remediation_batch_delivered_at:
            steps.append("remediation_delivery")
        if not self.remediation_validated_at:
            steps.append("validation")
        if not self.ingestion_unblocked_at:
            steps.append("unblock")
        if not self.postmortem_completed_at:
            steps.append("postmortem")
        return steps

The missing_steps() method is called by the escalation manager on a periodic schedule. If a step is missing and its due time has passed (computed from the detected_at timestamp plus the step's due_offset_minutes), the escalation manager sends a reminder or escalates to the next contact in the escalation path. This is the mechanism that prevents breach remediation from stalling silently.
Ingestion Blocking and Unblocking Logic

When a P1 breach triggers an ingestion block, the AI pipeline must maintain a clear record of what is blocked, why, and when the block was applied. Unblocking must be gated on successful validation of the remediation batch, not on the producer's claim that the issue is resolved.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class BlockState:
    contract_id: str
    blocked_since: datetime
    breach_event_id: str
    breach_type: str
    blocked_by: str  # Component that triggered the block
    unblocked_at: datetime | None = None
    remediation_batch_id: str | None = None

class IngestionBlockManager:
    def __init__(self, notify_fn: Callable[[str, str], None]):
        self._blocks: dict[str, BlockState] = {}  # contract_id -> BlockState
        self._notify = notify_fn

    def block(self, contract_id: str, breach_event_id: str, breach_type: str) -> None:
        if contract_id in self._blocks:
            return  # Already blocked; do not overwrite existing block
        state = BlockState(
            contract_id=contract_id,
            blocked_since=datetime.now(timezone.utc),
            breach_event_id=breach_event_id,
            breach_type=breach_type,
            blocked_by="ContractMonitor",
        )
        self._blocks[contract_id] = state
        self._notify("P1", f"Ingestion blocked for {contract_id}: {breach_type}")

    def is_blocked(self, contract_id: str) -> bool:
        return contract_id in self._blocks

    def attempt_unblock(
        self,
        contract_id: str,
        remediation_batch_id: str,
        validation_passed: bool,
        validation_violations: list[str],
    ) -> bool:
        if not validation_passed:
            self._notify(
                "P1",
                f"Remediation batch {remediation_batch_id} failed validation: "
                f"{len(validation_violations)} violations. Block remains."
            )
            return False
        block = self._blocks.pop(contract_id)
        block.unblocked_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
        block.remediation_batch_id = remediation_batch_id
        self._notify(
            "INFO",
            f"Ingestion unblocked for {contract_id} after remediation batch {remediation_batch_id}"
        )
        return True

The guard against overwriting an existing block prevents a second breach notification from resetting the block timestamp and losing the original breach event reference. This matters for audit: the block state records exactly when the P1 breach started and which batch eventually resolved it.
Remediation Batch Ingestion

A remediation batch is not a normal delivery. It covers a specific breach time window and must be treated differently from a regular incremental batch. The ingestion pipeline must handle several edge cases that do not arise with normal deliveries.

Overlap with already-indexed records: The remediation batch may contain records that were already indexed before the breach (pre-breach state). The pipeline must only update records whose version in the remediation batch is newer than the version currently in the vector store. A naive full-overwrite of the remediation batch can corrupt records that were correctly indexed before the breach.

Gap detection: After ingesting the remediation batch, run a reconciliation check for the entities that should have been in the breach window. Verify that every entity that had a change event during the breach window is now represented in the vector store with a version dated after the breach start. Flag any entities that remain un-remediated for manual investigation.

Remediation metadata tagging: Tag each chunk ingested from the remediation batch with a remediation flag and the breach event ID in the vector store metadata. This enables future audits to identify which chunks were produced by remediation batches versus normal deliveries, which matters for root cause analysis of persistent data quality issues.
Postmortem and Preventive Measures

Every P1 breach must produce a joint postmortem within 48 hours of resolution. The postmortem is the mechanism that prevents repeat breaches and drives continuous improvement in data pipeline reliability. A postmortem without corrective actions and a timeline is a document review exercise, not an improvement process.

Root cause categorization: Breaches should be categorized by root cause type (infrastructure failure, code bug, operational error, capacity exhaustion, third-party dependency) and tracked in a shared registry. Repeated breaches from the same root cause category indicate a systemic issue that requires architectural rather than operational remediation.

Corrective action ownership: Each corrective action in the postmortem must have a named owner and a concrete deadline. Corrective actions without owners are routinely forgotten. The data steward tracks corrective action completion and follows up before the deadline.

Contract term review: After a breach reveals that the current SLA terms are unrealistic given the producer's system constraints, the postmortem is the right time to renegotiate terms. Maintaining SLA terms that the producer cannot reliably meet produces a cycle of repeated breaches and postmortems without actual improvement.

Automated regression tests: Each breach should produce at least one new automated test that would have detected the breach sooner or prevented it. If the breach was caused by an undeclared schema change, add a schema compatibility test to the producer's CI pipeline. If it was caused by a completeness drop, add a completeness monitoring alert at a tighter threshold.
Key Takeaways

Breach severity determines the automated response: schema violations trigger P1 hard block; completeness below threshold triggers P2 warn-but-ingest with field-level flagging; latency breaches trigger staleness metadata and producer incident for repeated violations.

The remediation workflow has seven steps with defined owners and time limits, from 5-minute breach notification to 48-hour postmortem; all steps must be documented in the contract before a breach occurs.
Ingestion unblocking must be gated on successful validation of the remediation batch; the producer's claim that the issue is resolved is not sufficient.
Remediation batches require special handling: version-aware upsert to avoid overwriting correctly-indexed records, gap detection to verify full coverage, and remediation metadata tagging for audit.
Postmortems must produce named corrective actions with deadlines; breaches categorized by root cause type enable systemic fixes for repeat failure patterns.
